title: Taiwan Government Expresses Concern After China Labels Island 'Biggest Risk' in US-China Relations
summary: Taiwan's Ministry of Foreign Affairs voiced concern over China's characterization of Taiwan as the 'biggest risk' in bilateral ties during the Wang Yi-Rubio phone call, citing Beijing's continued escalation of pressure on the island.
contents:
## Taiwan Responds to Beijing's Threatening Rhetoric

### Official Response
Taiwan's Ministry of Foreign Affairs expressed concern hours after the Wang Yi-Rubio phone call, stating it found "threatening remarks on the Taiwan issue" in China's press release troubling. The ministry warned that Beijing had "recently continued to escalate pressure on Taiwan."

### Diplomatic Disruption Evidence
Taiwan pointed to recent incidents demonstrating Chinese pressure, including the alleged disruption of President Lai Ching-te's planned visit to Eswatini in late April. Three countries withdrew overflight permission due to Chinese pressure, forcing postponement of the African trip.

### Summit Implications
The Taiwan issue stands to dominate the planned May 14-15 Trump-Xi summit in Beijing. Trump previously suggested discussing Taiwan arms sales with Xi, a statement that generated significant concern in Taipei about potential deal-making involving the island's security.

### Military Pressure Continues
China has maintained near-daily military activity near Taiwan, sending warships and military aircraft in what analysts describe as a sustained campaign of coercion. Wang Yi's explicit framing of Taiwan as the "biggest point of risk" signals Beijing's intent to press the issue at the summit.

### Regional Reaction
China neither confirmed nor denied the allegations about disrupting the Eswatini visit but expressed "high appreciation" for countries adhering to the one-China principle.
